sentences.text: I was born as Majer Rozental.
That's the way we used to say our name.
M-A-J-E-R.
That's the first name.
Last name, R-O-Z-E-N-T-A-L.
Now, I am Mark Rosenthal.
So spell it?
M-A-R-K-R-O-S-E-N-T-H-A-L.
No.
I was born in Plock.
You spell it P-L-O-C-K.
Where the Vistula, the river Vistula. the main river of Poland.
Oh yeah, what date?
On December 17.
Yes.
Yes.
As a little boy, I remember my father was a woodcarver, mainly working for those carpenters, carving furniture.
Not for the poor people, of course, for people who got money, therefore.
But what happened, he used to be a good carver, and used to do very nice work.
But I remember it started in 1928, '29, they switched from those carved furniture into just plain.
They used to say that carved furniture is a dust collector.
And you know at that time, they didn't have those dusters like you're having now.
And it was bad.
And at that-- I was the youngest.
We were the three brothers.
The oldest was Szulim, the middle one was Nissan, and I was the youngest.
And we all wanted, the parents wanted to give us a good education.
But the way it happened, my father couldn't give it to us because it was very tough.
And my brother instead to go to a-- over the public-- he finished the public school, but he wanted to study, but no way.
Then he went to a watch maker and learned the trade.
He was there about nine months, but he was very good in this trade.
And at that time, when it-- for a-- to a beginner, they didn't pay.
And we needed the money.
Then my brother moved to Warsaw and immediately, they paid him.
But not too much.
But even with the very little pay, he used to save.
And yes, he used to send money-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Excuse me?
The older brother.
And the same thing happened with the middle brother, three years later.
Then what happened, the same thing.
He went also to a watch maker.
They didn't pay him, and he moved to Warsaw, rented a room, like the older brother.
And my father still alive on the income from the savings from that-- my brothers.
Oh, they left very young.
My brother could be at that time maybe 15? 15 and a half?
And they went to Warsaw just to make a living for themselves and-- and for-- for the parents.
Yes, yes, yes.
It means stomach money.
My father was, his name-- first name was Fajwel.
F-A-J-W-E-L.
And the mother was Ita.
I-T-A.
Yes.
And she used to have family in smaller towns about-- about 40 miles away.
Kilometers, I remember exactly, 63 kilometer.
Little town, a few streets, and they were in a better shape because they used to have a store.
And-- but-- During when I was very young, in the summer, during the vacation, I used to go there, for two months almost staying there.
And-- and my aunt used to take care of myself like-- like-- like her own son.
At that time, when I used to go there?
Nine years, ten years, eleven years.
I used to go there to that little town.
No, she was just a housewife.
No, at this time you couldn't get a job.
Women, no, no trade, nothing.
Mostly men used to work.
Very good.
Very close.
Very close.
They were very devoted.
They were devoted, and we were devoted.
And sometimes mother used to get sick.
No way to get a doctor.
No money for a doctor.
And the kids used to, we used to cry because couldn't help.
It was tough.
A tough life.
An apartment, yes, on the second floor.
Very poor.
We used to have at that time electricity, but we used to have just one bulb.
It was the time when they switched from those to electric bulb.
It was just one bulb.
And around 12 or 11 o'clock, the company used to give three signals and this was the sign that for the night, no lights.
Until seven o'clock in the morning.
We used to have, I would say, two rooms and a kitchen.
Yes, small.
And in the same apartment, my father used to have his-- his place to work.
He worked in the same apartment.
He carved in the same, yes.
He had a special, like any-- like any carpenter.
He used to have a special table and those special instruments to carve.
And he was a good carver.
He used to make very nice, nice furniture.
I was-- My oldest brother was more inclined.
He was-- I-- I-- maybe I was not.
I was inclined to other things than carving.
Like photography, but not in Plock.
When I-- When our family moved to Warsaw, when I made the first money, I bought a camera, and I used to take pictures.
In Plock, yes.
I went to two schools.
To the public school, and to a business school.
It was-- it was just for boys, and just for Jewish boys.
The other school, the business school, was mixed, which means, non-Jews, mostly, and some, a few of them, Jews.
To the school?
No.
I used to go by myself.
It was a small, not a big town.
The population was about 30,000.
I used to go by myself, never with my brothers.
I used to fight sometimes with my brothers.
But never, they never used to go-- they never used to go with me to the school.
I don't remember exactly, but you know, like boys.
All the time, something came out.
But we used to fight.
Not so often, but we used to fight.
Yes.
I remember we studied first of all, the Polish language.
Then we used to-- arithmetic, history, Polish literature.
I don't know how to-- with a pen--
What?
No.
Not pictures.
And what else?
In the public school, that's mostly what we used to, they used to teach us.
In the school?
No.
No.
At that time, nothing about the United-- we knew it's a rich country.
But they didn't teach us about the United States.
Yes.
At that time, yes.
My father especially.
The synagogue was almost across the street.
And on a Saturday, he would go definitely.
On Friday night and Saturday.
During the week he would pray at home three times-- three time daily.
Yeah.
And the mother, she didn't pray, but she would light candles on Friday night.
Oh yes.
Exactly.
That was on Saturdays, especially, when we were very young.
Later it was a little bit different, but we used to go.
Like anything, at that time, we used to be big believers.
That's a normal thing.
You're a Jew, you have to attend, you have to go to a school, pray, and pray to God for your health, for your parents' health, family health.
No.
No, no.
No religious, no.
I learned before I went to public school.
I went to Cheder.
I don't know whether you know about it.
It was, you know very primitive, and I used to learn how to pray.
I started, I believe, probably at the age of four and I went through the Cheder until age of seven.
And then I went to the public school, which was free, completely free.
For the business school, I got to pay.
To pay 25 zlotys.
It means, like today, I don't know maybe around $100 a month.
Yes.
But we didn't have the money, my oldest brother used to help me out.
If you didn't pay on time, they told you to leave the school and go back home.
Until you got the money, then you could attend the school again.
Yeah, it was tough.
Oh, yes.
We played mostly, we used to call it football, but in the United States we'd say soccer.
This was a very popular game.
And we didn't have even the proper ball.
We used to make ball from materials. it wasn't-- to buy a football, it was expensive.
You couldn't afford.
But-- but we used to play.
In any place.
Where ever there was room for it, we used to play.
My friends, they're the same age.
But we used to have, the real soccer, it used to be a Jewish organization like Maccabi.
And they had good players, trained players.
They used to subsidize.
And they used to play with some non-Jews in the stadiums.
Strange.
Not close.
Not close.
It was strange.
Polish people, they didn't like to get close.
I would say it was anti-Semitism.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
I remember when I was very, very young, and I went to a friend who used to be in good shape, materialistically, financially.
And it was at the time, it was before the Passover.
And all of a sudden, I walked over to his building, and this little boy, he was maybe three years younger than me, he started to cry.
Mother, Mother, the Jew came in to get me for the blood, for the matzos.
Because it used to be such legend.
That the Jewish, for matzos, for Passover, they're mixing with Christian blood.
Yeah.
And of course I was very disappointed.
I ran away back.
I didn't visit my friend, because, you know, I got scared and went back home.
But those accidents happened very often.
The Polish people would believe that the Jews are using kids' blood mixing for the Passover matzos.
Oh, of course it was never, never happened, but that's what's going on.
Sometimes they used to, if you would come to a Polish sections, they would sometimes hit you.
Or say unpleasant words.
Listen to this.
They would keep us, especially in the business school, separately.
We didn't sit together with the Christian boys.
We used to sit separately and they used to sit separately.
They didn't mix with us.
Of course, we used to talk, but very loose.
We used to mostly with our friends, with Jewish boys, we used to have conversations.
The teachers were more or less liberal.
But the kids brought from the home, you know, and not to mix in too much with the-- the Jewish boys.
To be a bookkeeper.
Here you say accountant, but in our-- our language, it was a bookkeeper.
To-- to-- to get a good profession.
We used to-- Not a trade, it's a profession.
And as a bookkeeper, you could make more money.
Good money, if you could get a job.
But you-- as a Jew, you couldn't get a job, any government job, or a state or a city job.
You could a get a job into another Jew's office, because they would never get you into.
I know my friends, non-Jews, they used to get those jobs, and they used to get any job.
In a bank or in the city hall, but none of us.
I used to struggle.
The first year, I believe I was completely without any work.
Then my family moved to Warsaw.
It was very tough.
And then I got a job through my uncle's-- my uncles brother-in-law.
He was a doctor, a radiologist.
And I got into this company where they used to sell those-- those x-ray supplies to the hospitals and the x-ray doctors.
Then I used to make money.
I used to support.
Any my oldest-- And then, when I could support my parents, my oldest-- oldest-- oldest brother got married.
He-- he used to go out with that girl for many years.
But he wouldn't marry her because-- because of the circumstances.
Until me and the middle brother could support the parents, then he got married.
Oh my brother got married maybe 1936?
No, '37 I believe.
The oldest--
--the oldest.
That's only one who got married before the war.
Szulim.
S-Z-U-L-I-M.
Yes.
No.
I used to have just one grandmother, who used to be the mother of my father.
There was no husband.
She used to go occasionally from one son, daughter, son, in different towns.
She never-- she didn't have her own place to live.
She used to live just with her son or with her daughter.
Excuse me?
No.
We had too small-- our apartment was too small to accommodate her.
Anything in my mind.
I wanted to go to study.
I wanted to, but I couldn't make it.
I couldn't make it.
I wanted very badly.
It's college.
Yes.
They used to teach accounting, even Polish, and literature, history, and stenography.
Typing.
And-- how to-- how-- how-- A lot about business.
How you make the merchandise.
We used to go to see different factories, to see the way certain products are produced.
I can say it just in Polish.
Szkola Handlowa.
To spell it?
S-Z-K-O-L-A, that means school.
And Handlowa, which means business.
Which means H-A-N-D-L-O-W-A.
Szkola Handlowa.
No, not in Plock.
In Warsaw.
At that time, we were in Warsaw--
--because what happened, my parents moved already, and I stayed one more year in Plock to finish the school.
And we made an arrangement that one from Plock stayed in our apartment in Warsaw, and I stayed at the guy's family apartment.
Because at that time, it was not for pay, just exchange.
An exchange.
That would be Kruk.
But you spell it not-- K-R-U-K.
Here you would say, C-R-O-O-K.
But Kruk, K-R-U-K.
Yeah, we considered it, but we figured we'd get a job in a business where the owner is a Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Oh, yes.
You see, this was September 1, 1939.
And on the second day, we could hear and see those planes coming into Warsaw, bombing.

And on the sixth of September 1939, the president of the city of Warsaw declared that all the young-- over the radio because at that time there was no television, just a radio-- that the young people should leave Warsaw, because Hitler's army is very close and to go to the East.
East, which means in the direction of Soviet Union.
The president of the city of-- of Warsaw.
And I called my oldest brother, and he came in and we-- and we start to go in that direction.
And on the highways there were tens of thousands of people.
It was terrible.
It was no traffic, nothing could move.
People who owned cars would sell their cars for a bicycle, because they couldn't get any gas.
And at the same time, the German planes, they would shoot down from the planes.
And we would try to go to get some cover, but was no cover.
And you got be lucky enough to get killed.
And it was a terrible, terrible-- the way it was.
But after-- this used to take, let's say, a few minutes.
And then, we start to walk again, and walked until we came to a little town like Radom, which took us also about two days.
It was about 100 kilometer, which was in miles, about 60 miles.
Yeah.
Me and my-- but I lost him the first day.
It was so many people, you know, that we lost each other.
What?
The younger, the middle brother, stayed with our uncle who was sick.
And he used to work for him, and he didn't-- he didn't want to leave, you know.
And he wanted to stay with his sick uncle.
In Warsaw.
Yeah.
Yeah, the parents stayed also.
But he didn't want to go.
Discussion was just the-- the-- the statement over the-- the radio, that to leave Warsaw.
That it's very dangerous to stay-- to stay in Warsaw.
That we shouldn't stay.
Young people shouldn't stay.
